article.title: Apple Launches 599 Dollar MacBook Neo in Biggest Push Yet Into Budget Laptop Market
article.summary: Apple has released the MacBook Neo, a $599 laptop powered by the A18 Pro chip, marking its most aggressive move into the budget PC segment and drawing competitive concern from Windows PC manufacturers.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Apple on March 4 [announced the MacBook Neo](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/say-hello-to-macbook-neo/), a $599 laptop that represents the company's most significant entry into the budget computing market. The device shipped on March 11 and is the first production Mac to use an A-series chip from the iPhone lineup rather than the M-series processors found in other Apple silicon Macs. At $400 less than the current MacBook Air, the Neo undercuts every new-generation laptop Apple has sold before.

## What We Know

The MacBook Neo is powered by the [A18 Pro chip](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/say-hello-to-macbook-neo/) with a six-core CPU, five-core GPU, and 16-core Neural Engine. Apple claims the chip delivers up to 50 percent faster performance in everyday tasks compared to an Intel Core Ultra 5 and up to three times faster on-device AI workloads. The machine ships with 8 GB of unified memory and a 256 GB SSD in the base configuration, with a 512 GB option available at $699.

The 13-inch Liquid Retina display runs at 2408 by 1506 pixels with 500 nits of brightness and support for one billion colors. Apple says the device delivers [up to 16 hours of battery life](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/say-hello-to-macbook-neo/), though independent testing by Engadget measured [12 hours and 15 minutes in a 4K video loop test](https://www.engadget.com/computing/laptops/macbook-neo-review-apple-puts-every-600-windows-pc-to-shame-130000878.html), well below the 18 hours achieved by the M4 MacBook Air but still sufficient for a full work or school day.

The device features a fanless aluminum design available in four colors: blush, indigo, silver, and citrus. Connectivity includes two USB-C ports, a headphone jack, Wi-Fi 6E, and Bluetooth 6. It weighs 2.7 pounds and includes a 1080p FaceTime HD camera, dual microphones, and dual side-firing speakers with Spatial Audio and Dolby Atmos.

John Ternus, Apple's senior vice president of Hardware Engineering, said the MacBook Neo is ["a laptop only Apple could create,"](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2026/03/say-hello-to-macbook-neo/) describing it as built from the ground up to be affordable for a broader audience. An education-tier price of $499 is also available.

The competitive response has been notable. ASUS CFO Nick Wu told Fortune that the MacBook Neo represents ["a shock to the entire market,"](https://fortune.com/2026/03/12/apple-macbook-neo-personal-computer-pc-asus-cfo-nick-wu-csuite-big-tech/) adding that all PC vendors, including upstream partners like Microsoft, Intel, and AMD, are actively discussing how to respond. Wu characterized the device as primarily designed for content consumption, noting it ["feels more like a tablet"](https://fortune.com/2026/03/12/apple-macbook-neo-personal-computer-pc-asus-cfo-nick-wu-csuite-big-tech/) due to its hardware limitations.

## What We Don't Know

It remains unclear how deeply Apple plans to invest in the Neo product line going forward, including whether a larger-screen variant or upgraded configurations are planned. The long-term impact on Apple's own MacBook Air sales is also uncertain, since the Neo occupies a price tier that could cannibalize demand for the company's existing entry-level offerings.

The extent to which Windows PC manufacturers will adjust pricing or product strategies in response is still developing. While ASUS has acknowledged the competitive pressure, concrete countermeasures from major OEMs have not yet been announced.

## Analysis

The MacBook Neo follows Apple's established pattern of expanding into price-sensitive segments after building brand equity at the premium end, similar to its approach with the iPhone SE and the entry-level iPad. By deploying the A18 Pro, a chip already amortized through iPhone production, Apple can offer competitive performance at a price point that challenges not only Windows laptops but also Chromebooks in the education and casual computing markets.

The trade-offs are deliberate. The absence of a backlit keyboard, the use of 8 GB of memory as a ceiling rather than a floor, and the lack of True Tone are concessions that preserve margin while keeping the device functional for its target audience. Reviewers at Engadget noted that [the Neo does not feel like an afterthought](https://www.engadget.com/computing/laptops/macbook-neo-review-apple-puts-every-600-windows-pc-to-shame-130000878.html), with build quality, trackpad, and screen quality exceeding expectations for its price class.

For the broader PC industry, the Neo poses a strategic question: whether to compete on price, which erodes already thin margins, or to differentiate on features that Apple has chosen to omit. The coming quarters will reveal whether the Neo reshapes budget laptop market dynamics or settles into a niche within Apple's lineup.
